summary: Jeff Atwood catalogs the most common and infuriating mistakes he witnessed speakers making at a VSLive conference, presenting them as satirical 'tips' for how not to give a presentation. He criticizes self-promotion, excessive agendas, bullet-point overload, reading slides verbatim, and ignoring time limits. The post argues that professional speakers should know better than to commit these basic errors. His frustration stems from the gap between what speakers are paid to do and their actual performance.
key_points: Professional speakers frequently commit basic presentation mistakes that undermine their credibility
Presentations should show rather than tell—demonstrations beat bullet points
Slides packed with text and read verbatim insult the audience's intelligence
Self-promotion and detailed agendas waste precious presentation time
Respecting time limits and taking questions shows respect for the audience
money_quotes: They get paid to present to large groups because they're ostensibly good communicators.
Don't show the audience. Tell them.
Audiences can't read. It's your responsibility to do all the reading for them.
The audience can wait. While you're fixing things up, try that NASCAR joke again. It's hilarious.
The content and quality of your presentation speaks for itself.
This stuff would be funny if it wasn't happening every single day.
key_insight: The most common presentation sins—self-promotion, text-heavy slides, and reading verbatim—all stem from making the presentation about the speaker rather than the audience.
